author: @bikerfirefarter7280
text:
Over-thought that, Luke. Not sure what advantage any of that is. Or you could just use a straw instead of dowel. Or roll your own 'tube' and use that as a core. Just rolling any fabric/paper between flat paddles automatically tightens the core until it is as dense as you want it. With two or three 'parallel' rollers set at a slight angle you can continuously feed in pasted paper so it spirals around itself and extrude unlimited length of tube/stick of any desired density/dia. And using two or more fabrics you can form concentric layers; e.g. soft low density core with hard high density sheath. etc. Heck, with a little practice you can do 'continuous' tube/rod just using the table/paddle method. You can even make tapering tubes/rods.
I'd like to see you demonstrate some/all of those methods.

author: @docink6175
text: many years ago I rolled hundreds of papers by wetting one edge of a newspaper sheet about 2 inches and wrapping that over a small dowel and rolling it up like you roll dough, wet the end to finish. every 10 rolls or so you could pull the dowels and reuse them. This is an interesting method but Im not sure its easier/faster

text: The real question is is it stronger than a wooden dowel of the same circumference as the stick and is it strong enough for the a signed job.  I'm thinking it might be stronger, especially if the grain of the paper is opposite the grain of the wood

author: @stevetobias4890
text: Most of the strength of the paper stick comes from the wood dowel centre. I actually thought you would remove the dowel once you got it started so you wouldn't need a new dowel for each stick.
